straight_beat.name: Don Pellegrino
straight_beat.column:
The Mets lost again, and the ledger keeps getting heavier. Yesterday’s 4-0 drubbing by the Dodgers wasn’t just another loss—it was a reminder of how fragile this team’s identity has become. You can’t blame the weather, or the umpires, or the fact that the sun was shining so hard it made the infield look like a spotlight. No, this is about the Mets not playing like a team that’s supposed to be in the thick of the playoff race.  

The pitching staff, for all its promise, continues to act like a group of kids playing a video game for the first time. Last night, the starters were supposed to be the backbone, the guys who could anchor a rotation that’s supposed to be competitive. Instead, they looked like a collection of

optimist.name: Sheila 'Sheesh' Fortunato
optimist.column:
The Mets lost yesterday, 0-4 to the Dodgers, and now the record reads 7-10, 3.0 games behind in the NL East. Yeah, I know. It’s the kind of day that makes you want to throw your pizza dough in the air and scream, “Ya gotta believe, no lie!” But here’s the thing: this team’s got a recipe that’s been simmering since the first pitch of spring training. It’s not about the scoreboard right now. It’s about the fire in the belly of the players, the front office’s vision, and the fact that every loss is just the oven preheating for the next batch of magic.  

Let’s talk about the Polar Bear. That guy, the one with the swagger and the swing, is out there every day, grinding like he’s got a pizza oven in his soul. He’s not just a hitter—he’s a statement. And when you’ve got a guy like that, you’ve got a chance to make the other team’s manager scream into his napkin. The Dodgers might have had their way yesterday, but that’s just the wind before the storm.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Sheesh, the Mets are struggling, and you’re still smiling like it

pessimist.name: Sal Marusiak
pessimist.column: The Mets lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your TV remote into a wall. Four runs, zero resistance. The kind of performance that makes you wonder if the team’s DNA is coded with the word “collapse.” You think back to the days when the Mets were a contender, when they had a chance to make noise in the playoffs.
